refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validate p95 latency across 1000+ control cycles under worst-case network conditions within the agreed envelope.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with minimal latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received control commands on vehicle actuators with deterministic response time
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report communication link health and latency metrics to operator and safety system
FR-TEL-006: Gracefully degrade or halt teleoperation if latency exceeds safe thresholds

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency network stack with priority queuing, adaptive compression, and edge processing to minimize sensor-to-operator transit time; use deterministic vehicle control firmware with bounded actuator response times
DP-B: Deploy dedicated high-bandwidth, low-latency network infrastructure (5G/private network) with local edge compute nodes for sensor preprocessing and command buffering

DP-C: Hybrid approach combining optimized network protocols (UDP-based, minimal overhead), local sensor fusion and command prediction at vehicle, and remote operator interface with latency compensation (predictive display, command buffering)
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hybrid latency-optimized architecture comprising: (1) lightweight UDP-based telemetry protocol with adaptive payload compression and priority queuing at the vehicle; (2) local sensor fusion and predictive command execution on vehicle firmware to mask network jitter; (3) remote operator interface with latency-aware feedback (predicted vehicle state display, command buffering with acknowledgment); (4) continuous latency monitoring and safety interlock that halts teleoperation if p95 latency exceeds 150 ms threshold.

potential_sources: Network infrastructure design (DP-C depends on agreed network conditions; coupling to network provisioning decisions)
Vehicle firmware architecture (DP-C requires deterministic control loop; coupling to vehicle control system design)
Operator interface design (DP-C requires latency-aware UX; coupling to HMI/tele-operator workstation design)
Safety system integration (DP-C requires safety interlock; coupling to Safety Services module)
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is decoupled from specific network technology (5G vs. private vs. hybrid) and allows graceful degradation. Coupling is manageable via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) are agreed and characterized before design finalization.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle firmware can support deterministic control loops with bounded response times (< 50 ms typical).
ASSUMPTION: Operator acceptance of predictive display and command buffering as latency compensation mechanisms.
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed envelope, latency target may not be achievable; mitigation via safety interlock.
RISK: Predictive command execution may diverge from operator intent if network delays are highly variable; mitigation via continuous feedback and operator override.
RISK: Adaptive compression may introduce additional latency or data loss; mitigation via protocol tuning and testing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify network condition assumptions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles) and document agreed envelope. Define latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, network transit, and actuator response. Specify safety interlock behavior (e.g., return-to-safe-state vs. operator-controlled halt).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication and control logic, including: (1) vehicle-side telemetry acquisition and transmission (sensor fusion, compression, UDP protocol); (2) remote operator-side command reception and transmission (latency monitoring, predictive display, command buffering); (3) latency measurement and safety interlock logic; (4) graceful degradation and fallback behaviors.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits sensor telemetry (position, orientation, video, proximity, latency metrics) to remote operator via Telemetry interface. Payload optimized for low latency; adaptive compression applied.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator control commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) via Tasking interface. Commands buffered and executed with deterministic timing on vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits safety alerts (latency threshold exceeded, communication link lost, graceful degradation triggered) to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link interface for bidirectional communication with remote operator. Latency and jitter characteristics of Network Link directly impact teleoperation performance.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of latency-critical telemetry and command logic. All sensor acquisition, compression, protocol handling, and latency monitoring are contained. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link.

Vehicle module owns vehicle control firmware and actuators, but teleoperation-specific logic (latency compensation, predictive execution, safety interlock) should not be scattered across Vehicle. High diffusion risk if latency logic is embedded in Vehicle firmware without clear separation.

Safety Services owns safety interlocks and alerts, but latency monitoring and telemetry protocol are not safety-specific concerns. Assigning latency logic to Safety Services would create high diffusion and coupling.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning latency logic here would bypass architectural layering and create diffusion across multiple sub-systems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it encapsulates all latency-critical telemetry and command logic; (2) it owns the Telemetry, Tasking, and Network Link interfaces; (3) it minimizes diffusion by centralizing latency monitoring and adaptive protocols; (4) it maintains clear separation of concerns from Vehicle (control firmware) and Safety Services (safety interlocks).
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle firmware is not designed to support deterministic control loops, latency compensation logic in Teleoperation may be ineffective. Mitigation: establish Vehicle firmware requirements and interface contracts.
RISK: If Network Link interface does not expose latency metrics, Teleoperation cannot monitor p95 latency. Mitigation: ensure Network Link interface includes latency telemetry.
RISK: If Safety Services does not respond promptly to SafetyAlert (latency threshold exceeded), safety interlock may fail. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert response time requirements and testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Vehicle module provides deterministic control loop with bounded response time (< 50 ms). Confirm that Network Link interface exposes latency metrics. Define SafetyAlert interface contract and response time requirements for Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid latency-optimized architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (teleoperation latency ≤ 150 ms p95). DP-C includes all necessary mechanisms: low-latency protocol, adaptive compression, local sensor fusion, predictive display, latency monitoring, and safety interlock. No decoupling or independence issues detected. DP-C is feasible and testable.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If latency monitoring logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, it becomes difficult to maintain and verify. Mitigation: centralize latency monitoring in Teleoperation module with clear interface contracts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-C. All latency-critical logic (protocol, compression, predictive execution, monitoring) is owned by Teleoperation. External dependencies (Vehicle firmware, Network Link, Safety Services) are managed via well-defined interfaces. Containment is strong and diffusion is minimal.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-008 is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose latency metrics via Telemetry interface. Operator acceptance testing can validate p95 latency and safety interlock behavior.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-006) map to specific Teleoperation responsibilities: sensor acquisition, telemetry transmission, command reception, actuator execution, link health monitoring, and graceful degradation.

recommended_revisions: Establish and document agreed network condition envelope (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles) before design finalization.
Define latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition (Vehicle), network transit (Network Link), and actuator response (Vehicle).
Specify safety interlock behavior and response time requirements for Safety Services.
Confirm Vehicle firmware supports deterministic control loops with bounded response times.
Define Network Link interface contract to expose latency metrics and network health indicators.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies six sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-006) that address distinct aspects of teleoperation: sensor acquisition, telemetry transmission, command reception, actuator execution, link health monitoring, and graceful degradation. Each sub-FR requires its own FR-DP-IM decomposition to ensure complete coverage and traceability. DP-C and Teleoperation module assignment are sound and ready for next level, but sub-FRs must be refined and allocated to specific design parameters and implementation modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with minimal latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received control commands on vehicle actuators with deterministic response time
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report communication link health and latency metrics to operator and safety system
FR-TEL-006: Gracefully degrade or halt teleoperation if latency exceeds safe thresholds

assumptions: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) are agreed and characterized before design finalization.
Vehicle firmware supports deterministic control loops with bounded response times (< 50 ms typical).
Operator acceptance of predictive display and command buffering as latency compensation mechanisms.
Safety Services responds promptly to SafetyAlert signals (latency threshold exceeded).
Network Link interface exposes latency metrics and network health indicators.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed envelope, making latency target unachievable.
Predictive command execution may diverge from operator intent if network delays are highly variable.
Adaptive compression may introduce additional latency or data loss.
Vehicle firmware may not support deterministic control loops required for latency compensation.
Safety interlock may fail if Safety Services does not respond promptly to SafetyAlert.
evidence_needed: Agreed network condition envelope (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles) with justification.
Vehicle firmware specification confirming deterministic control loop and bounded response times.
Network Link interface specification including latency metrics and health indicators.
Safety Services specification including SafetyAlert response time requirements.
Operator acceptance test results validating p95 latency and safety interlock behavior under agreed network conditions.
End-to-end latency measurement data from prototype or simulation under representative scenarios.
